---
title: "The Dot Product: Length and Angle from Coordinates"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Geometry of Data"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from mpl_toolkits.mplot3d import Axes3D  # noqa: F401

# One restrained visual style for every figure in this article, matched to the
# rest of the Geometry-of-Data track.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

VEC    = "#2b3a67"   # vectors
HILITE = "#c1121f"   # the highlighted vector / quantity
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"   # angle / projection highlights
GREY   = "#6c757d"
PALE   = "#9fb3d1"


def draw_vec(ax, tail, head, color=VEC, lw=2.4, label=None, z=5,
             label_offset=(0.0, 0.0)):
    """Draw a 2-D arrow from tail to head with a clean head."""
    ax.annotate("", xy=head, xytext=tail,
                arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=color, lw=lw,
                                shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0), zorder=z)
    if label is not None:
        ax.text(head[0] + label_offset[0], head[1] + label_offset[1], label,
                color=color, fontsize=13, ha="center", va="center")
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the fourth article in the **Geometry of
> Data** track and the opening of Movement II, where length and angle finally enter the
> picture. It assumes [GEOM-00 *What Is a Vector, Really?*] (a vector is at once an arrow,
> a list of numbers in $\mathbb{R}^n$, and a row of a dataset) and [GEOM-01 *Adding and
> Scaling*] (the linear combination $c_1\mathbf{v}_1 + \cdots + c_k\mathbf{v}_k$). It
> also leans on one fact from below the Calc-1 floor — the **law of cosines** — which we
> restate and use, not assume. Everything so far has been *affine*: we could add and
> scale, but we had no way to say how *long* a vector is or what *angle* sits between two
> of them. This article installs both, and it does so from a single new operation, the
> **dot product**. It sets up [GEOM-05 *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*]
> (perpendicular means "dot product zero"), [GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection*] (the
> keystone, built entirely from dot products), and [GEOM-17 *Quadratic Forms*] (which
> generalize the length-squared $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}$).

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

A vector's coordinates are just numbers — they carry no ruler and no protractor. Yet from
those numbers alone we can recover two of the most geometric quantities imaginable: the
**length** of a vector and the **angle** between two of them. The bridge is one
deceptively small operation, the **dot product** $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \sum_i
x_i y_i$: multiply matching coordinates, add them up, get a single number. This article
defends one claim — that **this one scalar simultaneously encodes length and angle**, and
that the identity tying them together,
$$\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta,$$
is not a definition handed down from above but a *theorem* we derive from the law of
cosines. We build the length (norm) from the Pythagorean theorem in $n$ dimensions, prove
the angle formula, prove the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality that makes the angle well-defined,
and cash it all out as the **cosine similarity** that powers search engines and
recommender systems. The payoff is that "how aligned are these two data points?" becomes a
quantity we can compute from a spreadsheet.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
The **dot product** of two vectors is one number built by multiplying matching
coordinates and summing: $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = x_1 y_1 + \cdots + x_n y_n$. That
single number holds *all* the metric geometry. Set $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{x}$ and you get the
**squared length**, $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x} = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2$ — Pythagoras in
$n$ dimensions. Keep $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ distinct and you get the **angle**
between them, through $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} =
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta$. Length and angle — the entire
ruler-and-protractor kit — fall out of one sum of products.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The dot product will look at first like an arbitrary
recipe — *why* multiply coordinates and add? — but by the end it should feel inevitable:
it is the *only* operation, up to scaling, that turns the Pythagorean theorem and the law
of cosines into algebra you can run on a list of numbers. Once we have it, the central
data-science reading follows immediately: **a dot product measures how much two vectors
point the same way**, and "pointing the same way" is exactly what we mean when we say two
documents are similar, two users have the same taste, or two features are correlated.

# Why we are doing this: coordinates lost their geometry {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **we threw away the picture when we
turned vectors into lists of numbers — how do we get the geometry back?** Every step below
is an answer to some piece of that.

## What we had, and what we lost

[GEOM-00] made a vector three things at once: an arrow with a tip and a tail, a list of
numbers $(x_1,\dots,x_n)$, and a row of a data table. [GEOM-01] then gave us the only two
operations we were allowed: add two vectors (tip-to-tail), and scale a vector (stretch or
flip). With just those two moves we can build any linear combination, and that is enough
to talk about *which directions we can reach* (span, [GEOM-02]) and *whether our directions
are redundant* (independence, [GEOM-03]).

But notice what we still cannot do. We cannot say a vector is "three units long." We
cannot say two vectors are "perpendicular," or "almost parallel," or "$30^\circ$ apart."
Addition and scaling are **affine** operations — they respect the flat structure of space
(lines stay lines, the origin stays put) but they are blind to *distance* and *angle*. A
shear transformation, for instance, preserves all linear-combination structure yet
mangles every length and angle in sight. So far our space is a sheet of graph paper with
no ruler printed on it and no protractor in the drawer.

This is not a pedantic gap. It is the gap that blocks almost everything the rest of the
series wants to do:

- **Regression** asks for the line closest to a cloud of points — and "closest" is a
  distance.
- **Classification** separates groups by how *far apart* and how *aligned* their feature
  vectors are.
- **Clustering** groups points that are *near* each other.
- **Recommendation and search** rank items by how *similar* two vectors are.

Every one of those words — closest, far, aligned, near, similar — is a metric notion. None
of them is available from addition and scaling alone. We need to install length and angle.

## The original problem: from physics to a sum of products

Historically, the dot product did not arrive as an abstract definition. It arrived from
mechanics. When a force $\mathbf{F}$ pushes an object through a displacement $\mathbf{d}$,
the **work** done is not $\lVert\mathbf{F}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{d}\rVert$ — only the part of
the force that lies *along* the motion counts. Push a sled horizontally while pulling the
rope at an angle and only the horizontal component of your pull moves the sled; the
vertical part is wasted. The right quantity is
$\lVert\mathbf{F}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{d}\rVert\cos\theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle
between push and motion: full credit when you push straight along the motion ($\theta=0$,
$\cos\theta=1$), zero credit when you push sideways ($\theta=90^\circ$, $\cos\theta=0$),
and negative when you push backward ($\theta>90^\circ$).

In the 1840s W. R. Hamilton, building his quaternions, isolated exactly this
"projection-weighted product" of two directed quantities; by the 1880s J. W. Gibbs and
Oliver Heaviside had stripped the quaternion scaffolding away and left the clean operation
we now call the dot (or *scalar*, or *inner*) product, taught as a stand-alone tool of
vector analysis. The number it computes was *defined* geometrically — magnitudes times the
cosine of the angle — and only later recognized to equal the humble coordinate sum
$\sum_i x_i y_i$. The thread we will pull on is that recognition, **run in reverse**: we
will *start* from the coordinate sum (because that is what a computer has — a list of
numbers) and *recover* the geometry (length and angle) that the physicists started with.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Is the dot product a vector or a number?"* A number — a scalar. That is the whole point,
and it is why "scalar product" is a synonym. You feed in two vectors and get back one real
number. Contrast this with the **cross product** (a different operation, vectors in only),
which returns a vector and exists only in three dimensions. The dot product works in every
dimension, $\mathbb{R}^2$ or $\mathbb{R}^{10000}$, and always returns a single number. That
generality is exactly why it, not the cross product, is the workhorse of data science,
where vectors routinely live in thousands of dimensions.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Before any algebra, hold two pictures in mind.

**Length is the Pythagorean theorem, repeated.** In the plane, a vector $\mathbf{x} =
(x_1, x_2)$ is the hypotenuse of a right triangle with legs $x_1$ and $x_2$, so its length
is $\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}$ — that is just Pythagoras. In three dimensions you apply
Pythagoras twice (once in the base plane, once to climb to the tip) and get
$\sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2 + x_3^2}$. The pattern looks like it wants to continue — length is the
square root of the sum of squared coordinates, in any dimension — but "looks like" is not a
proof, so we do not lean on it: @sec-norm earns it with a full induction.

**Angle is about alignment.** Two arrows from the same origin can point the same way
($0^\circ$), opposite ways ($180^\circ$), at right angles ($90^\circ$), or anywhere
between. The dot product is going to read off where on that spectrum they sit — large and
positive when they agree, zero when perpendicular, negative when they oppose. @fig-align
shows the three regimes and previews the sign of the dot product in each.

```{python}
#| label: fig-align
#| fig-cap: 'The dot product reads alignment. Holding a reference vector $\mathbf{x}$ fixed (dark blue), a second vector $\mathbf{y}$ that points roughly the same way gives a *positive* dot product (left, acute angle); a perpendicular $\mathbf{y}$ gives *exactly zero* (middle); a vector pointing against $\mathbf{x}$ gives a *negative* dot product (right, obtuse angle). The sign of $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}$ is the sign of the cosine of the angle between them.'
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.2, 3.3))
x = np.array([2.4, 0.6])
configs = [
    (np.array([1.8, 1.6]),  "acute:  $\\mathbf{x}^\\top\\mathbf{y} > 0$"),
    (np.array([-0.5, 2.0]), "right:  $\\mathbf{x}^\\top\\mathbf{y} = 0$"),
    (np.array([-1.6, 1.4]), "obtuse: $\\mathbf{x}^\\top\\mathbf{y} < 0$"),
]
# Make the middle one exactly perpendicular to x.
configs[1] = (np.array([-x[1], x[0]]) * 0.85, configs[1][1])
for ax, (y, title) in zip(axes, configs):
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), x, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{x}$", label_offset=(0.25, -0.15))
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), y, color=HILITE, label=r"$\mathbf{y}$", label_offset=(0.1, 0.25))
    dp = float(x @ y)
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(-2.6, 3.2); ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 2.8)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
    ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.5)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()
```

The plan is now concrete. Define the dot product as the sum of products (@sec-def). Show
that setting both inputs equal gives squared length, recovering Pythagoras in $n$
dimensions (@sec-norm). Then prove — via the law of cosines — that the dot product of two
*different* vectors equals $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta$, which
hands us the angle (@sec-angle). Prove Cauchy–Schwarz, which guarantees that cosine is a
legal cosine (@sec-cauchy). Cash it out as cosine similarity on real data
(@sec-examples). Mark the boundary where the standard dot product is the *wrong* ruler
(@sec-failure).

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## The dot product, defined {#sec-def}

::: {#def-dot}
**Definition (dot product).** Let $\mathbf{x} = (x_1, \dots, x_n)$ and $\mathbf{y} =
(y_1, \dots, y_n)$ be two vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ — two lists of $n$ real numbers. Their
**dot product** is the single real number
$$
\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i y_i
\;=\; x_1 y_1 + x_2 y_2 + \cdots + x_n y_n .
$$ {#eq-dot-def}
:::

Read @eq-dot-def slowly. We march down the two lists in lockstep, multiply the entries
that sit in the same position ($x_1$ with $y_1$, $x_2$ with $y_2$, and so on), and add all
$n$ products. The output is **one number**, not a vector. The two inputs must have the
*same* length $n$, or there is nothing to pair $x_i$ with.

A word on notation, since several forms appear in the wild and we will use one
consistently. We write the dot product as $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}$ — a "row times a
column." The symbol $\mathbf{x}^\top$ is the **transpose** of the column vector
$\mathbf{x}$: it lays the column on its side into a row. Stacking a $1\times n$ row against
an $n \times 1$ column and using the row-times-column rule produces exactly the single sum
$\sum_i x_i y_i$. (The transpose gets its own full treatment in [GEOM-10]; here we use it
only as compact notation for "pair up and sum," and the matrix-multiplication rule that
justifies it is built in [GEOM-09].) The older notation $\mathbf{x}\cdot\mathbf{y}$ — the
literal "dot" — means the identical number; the most general name, $\langle
\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\rangle$, the **inner product**, we save for @sec-inner where we say
what makes the dot product just *one* inner product among many.

Three algebraic properties fall straight out of the definition, and we will use all three
constantly, so we prove them now rather than assert them.

::: {#lem-dot-props}
**Lemma (algebra of the dot product).** For all vectors $\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y},\mathbf{z}
\in \mathbb{R}^n$ and every scalar $c \in \mathbb{R}$:

1. **Symmetry:** $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{x}$.
2. **Linearity in each slot:** $(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{z})^\top\mathbf{y} =
   \mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{z}^\top\mathbf{y}$, and $(c\mathbf{x})^\top\mathbf{y}
   = c\,(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y})$.
3. **Positivity:** $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x} = \sum_i x_i^2 \ge 0$, and it equals $0$ if
   and only if $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{0}$ (the zero vector).
:::

::: {.proof}
Each is a one-line consequence of the definition @eq-dot-def, which is built from ordinary
multiplication and addition of real numbers.

*Symmetry.* Real multiplication commutes, so $x_i y_i = y_i x_i$ for every $i$. Summing
both sides, $\sum_i x_i y_i = \sum_i y_i x_i$, i.e. $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} =
\mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{x}$.

*Linearity in the first slot.* The $i$-th entry of $\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{z}$ is $x_i + z_i$,
so by @eq-dot-def,
$$
(\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{z})^\top\mathbf{y}
= \sum_i (x_i + z_i)\,y_i
= \sum_i \bigl(x_i y_i + z_i y_i\bigr)
= \sum_i x_i y_i + \sum_i z_i y_i
= \mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} + \mathbf{z}^\top\mathbf{y},
$$
using the distributive law $(a+b)c = ac+bc$ inside the sum and then splitting the sum.
For the scaling part, the $i$-th entry of $c\mathbf{x}$ is $c x_i$, so
$(c\mathbf{x})^\top\mathbf{y} = \sum_i (c x_i) y_i = c\sum_i x_i y_i =
c\,(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y})$, pulling the constant $c$ out of the sum. By symmetry
(part 1) the same linearity holds in the *second* slot.

*Positivity.* Setting $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{x}$ in @eq-dot-def gives
$\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x} = \sum_i x_i x_i = \sum_i x_i^2$. A real square is never
negative, so the sum of squares is $\ge 0$. A sum of nonnegative terms is zero only when
*every* term is zero, i.e. $x_i^2 = 0$ for all $i$, i.e. $x_i = 0$ for all $i$, which is
exactly $\mathbf{x}=\mathbf{0}$. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Property 3 is the quiet hero of the whole article. It says $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}$ is a
nonnegative number that vanishes only at the origin — precisely the behavior we want from
a *squared length*. That is the next step.

## Length: Pythagoras in $n$ dimensions {#sec-norm}

We now define length and show it agrees with the Pythagorean picture, not just in two or
three dimensions but in all of them.

::: {#def-norm}
**Definition (norm / length).** The **norm** (or **length**, or **magnitude**) of a vector
$\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ is
$$
\lVert \mathbf{x}\rVert \;=\; \sqrt{\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}}
\;=\; \sqrt{\,\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i^2\,}.
$$ {#eq-norm-def}
:::

The square root is legitimate because @lem-dot-props(3) guarantees the thing under it,
$\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}$, is never negative. Squaring both sides records the relation we
will reach for over and over:
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 = \mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x} = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2 .
$$ {#eq-norm-sq}

But why is $\sqrt{\sum_i x_i^2}$ the *right* notion of length and not some other formula?
Because it is forced by the Pythagorean theorem, the one geometric fact we are willing to
take as given at the Calc-1 floor. Let us watch it build up dimension by dimension, since
the $n$-dimensional case is nothing but the 2-D case applied repeatedly.

**Two dimensions.** A vector $\mathbf{x}=(x_1,x_2)$ runs from the origin to the point
$(x_1,x_2)$. Drop a perpendicular to the horizontal axis and you have a right triangle:
one leg of length $|x_1|$ along the horizontal, one leg of length $|x_2|$ vertical, and
the vector itself as the hypotenuse. The Pythagorean theorem — *in a right triangle the
square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the legs* — gives
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 = x_1^2 + x_2^2 ,
\qquad\text{so}\qquad
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert = \sqrt{x_1^2 + x_2^2}.
$$
(We use $x_1^2$, not $|x_1|^2$; they are the same number, since squaring already discards
the sign.) This matches @eq-norm-def with $n=2$.

**Three dimensions.** Take $\mathbf{x}=(x_1,x_2,x_3)$. First find the length of its shadow
in the horizontal $(x_1,x_2)$-plane: by the 2-D case that shadow has length $r$ with
$r^2 = x_1^2 + x_2^2$. Now the full vector is the hypotenuse of a *second* right triangle,
whose horizontal leg is that shadow (length $r$) and whose vertical leg rises by $x_3$.
These two legs meet at a right angle (the vertical axis is perpendicular to the horizontal
plane), so Pythagoras applies again:
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 = r^2 + x_3^2 = (x_1^2 + x_2^2) + x_3^2 = x_1^2 + x_2^2 + x_3^2 .
$$
We applied the *same* theorem twice and the squares simply accumulated. @fig-pythagoras
draws this two-step climb.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pythagoras
#| fig-cap: 'Length in three dimensions is Pythagoras applied twice. The shadow of $\mathbf{x}$ in the floor plane has squared length $x_1^2 + x_2^2$ (first right triangle, in the floor). The vector $\mathbf{x}$ is then the hypotenuse of a second right triangle whose legs are that shadow and the vertical rise $x_3$, giving $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 = (x_1^2 + x_2^2) + x_3^2$. The same accumulation of squares continues into every higher dimension, even where we can no longer draw it.'
x1, x2, x3 = 2.6, 2.0, 2.4
fig = plt.figure(figsize=(6.0, 5.0))
ax = fig.add_subplot(111, projection="3d")

# The vector and its shadow.
ax.plot([0, x1], [0, x2], [0, 0], color=ACCENT, lw=2.2)             # shadow in floor
ax.plot([x1, x1], [x2, x2], [0, x3], color=GREY, lw=1.8, ls="--")  # vertical rise
ax.plot([0, x1], [0, x2], [0, x3], color=HILITE, lw=2.8)           # the vector x

# Floor triangle legs.
ax.plot([0, x1], [0, 0], [0, 0], color=VEC, lw=1.6)
ax.plot([x1, x1], [0, x2], [0, 0], color=VEC, lw=1.6)

ax.text(x1 * 0.5, x2 * 0.5, x3 * 0.55, r"$\mathbf{x}$", color=HILITE, fontsize=14)
ax.text(x1 * 0.55, x2 * 0.55, -0.35, "shadow $r$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=10)
ax.text(x1 + 0.15, x2 + 0.15, x3 * 0.5, "$x_3$", color=GREY, fontsize=11)
ax.text(x1 * 0.5, -0.45, 0, "$x_1$", color=VEC, fontsize=11)
ax.text(x1 + 0.2, x2 * 0.5, 0, "$x_2$", color=VEC, fontsize=11)

ax.set_xlim(0, 3); ax.set_ylim(0, 3); ax.set_zlim(0, 3)
ax.set_xlabel("axis 1"); ax.set_ylabel("axis 2"); ax.set_zlabel("axis 3")
ax.view_init(elev=20, azim=-58)
ax.set_title("Length is Pythagoras, applied once per new dimension")
plt.tight_layout()
```

**$n$ dimensions, by induction.** The three-dimensional argument exposes the general
machine. Suppose for some dimension $k$ we have shown that a vector
$(x_1,\dots,x_k)$ has squared length $x_1^2 + \cdots + x_k^2$ — that is our **induction
hypothesis**. Now take a vector $(x_1,\dots,x_k,x_{k+1})$ in $\mathbb{R}^{k+1}$. Its first
$k$ coordinates form a vector lying in the $\mathbb{R}^k$ "floor," whose squared length is
$x_1^2 + \cdots + x_k^2$ by the hypothesis. The new axis (coordinate $k+1$) is
perpendicular to that entire floor, so the full vector is the hypotenuse of a right
triangle with legs $\sqrt{x_1^2 + \cdots + x_k^2}$ and $x_{k+1}$. Pythagoras gives
$$
\lVert(x_1,\dots,x_{k+1})\rVert^2
= \bigl(x_1^2 + \cdots + x_k^2\bigr) + x_{k+1}^2
= x_1^2 + \cdots + x_k^2 + x_{k+1}^2 .
$$
This is the statement for dimension $k+1$. Since we verified the claim for $k=2$ outright,
it now holds for $k=3$, hence $k=4$, and so on through every dimension: by induction,
$\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 = \sum_{i=1}^n x_i^2$ in $\mathbb{R}^n$ for every $n$. The norm
of @eq-norm-def is therefore not a definition pulled from thin air — it is the only formula
consistent with Pythagoras holding in every dimension at once.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Isn't the higher-dimensional Pythagorean theorem itself something that needs proving —
aren't you assuming the thing you want?"* No, and the distinction matters. We assume
Pythagoras only for an ordinary **right triangle in a plane** — three points, one right
angle, the Calc-1-floor fact. The induction never uses a "higher Pythagoras"; it uses the
*planar* theorem repeatedly, each time in the 2-D plane spanned by the running diagonal and
one new axis. The reason a new axis is perpendicular to all the previous ones is the
defining feature of Cartesian coordinates: the axes are mutually perpendicular by
construction. So the only geometric input is the plane theorem, applied $n-1$ times.
:::

A vector of length $1$ is called a **unit vector**. Any nonzero $\mathbf{x}$ can be turned
into a unit vector pointing the same way by dividing by its length,
$\hat{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{x}/\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert$; this is called **normalizing**, and
we will lean on it heavily when we isolate *direction* from *magnitude* in @sec-examples.
That the result has length $1$ is immediate from @lem-dot-props(2): $\lVert
c\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 = (c\mathbf{x})^\top(c\mathbf{x}) = c^2 \mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x} = c^2
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2$, so $\lVert c\mathbf{x}\rVert = |c|\,\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert$, and
with $c = 1/\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert$ this is $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert /
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert = 1$. Scaling a vector scales its length by the same factor — exactly
what "stretch by $c$" should do.

## Distance falls out for free {#sec-distance}

Once we can measure length, distance comes at no extra cost. The **distance** between two
points $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ is the length of the arrow joining them, i.e. the norm
of their difference:
$$
\operatorname{dist}(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}) = \lVert\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}\rVert
= \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - y_i)^2}.
$$ {#eq-distance}
This is the familiar **Euclidean distance** — the straight-line distance you would measure
with a ruler — now defined in any number of dimensions purely from coordinates. The
$\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}$ arrow runs from the tip of $\mathbf{y}$ to the tip of $\mathbf{x}$
(tip-to-tail addition: $\mathbf{y} + (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}) = \mathbf{x}$), and its length
is what @eq-distance computes. Every "how close are these two data points?" question in
clustering and nearest-neighbor methods is this one formula.

## Angle: the dot product through the law of cosines {#sec-angle}

Now the centerpiece. We have length from $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}$; we want angle from
$\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}$. The tool is the **law of cosines**, the generalization of
Pythagoras to triangles that are *not* right-angled. We restate it (it sits just below our
floor) and then use it.

::: {.callout-note title="The law of cosines (restated, not assumed)"}
In any triangle with side lengths $a$, $b$, $c$, where $\theta$ is the angle *opposite*
the side of length $c$ (equivalently, the angle *between* the sides of lengths $a$ and
$b$),
$$
c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab\cos\theta .
$$
When $\theta = 90^\circ$, $\cos\theta = 0$ and this collapses to $c^2 = a^2 + b^2$ —
ordinary Pythagoras. The $-2ab\cos\theta$ term is the *correction* for the angle not being
a right angle: it subtracts when $\theta$ is acute (the opposite side is shorter than
Pythagoras would predict) and adds when $\theta$ is obtuse ($\cos\theta<0$).
:::

Set up the triangle. Place $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ as two arrows from the same
origin, with $\theta$ the angle between them. The third side of the triangle they form is
the vector joining their tips, $\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}$ (it runs from the tip of
$\mathbf{y}$ to the tip of $\mathbf{x}$). So the three side lengths are
$$
a = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert, \qquad b = \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert,
\qquad c = \lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert,
$$
and $\theta$ — between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ — is exactly the angle opposite side
$c$. @fig-lawcos draws this triangle.

```{python}
#| label: fig-lawcos
#| fig-cap: 'The triangle behind the angle formula. Two vectors $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ share a tail; the side joining their tips is $\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}$. The side lengths are $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert$, $\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$, and $\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert$, with the angle $\theta$ between $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ sitting opposite the third side. Applying the law of cosines to this triangle and expanding the lengths with the dot product yields $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta$.'
x = np.array([3.0, 0.7])
y = np.array([1.3, 2.5])
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(5.6, 4.4))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), x, color=VEC, label=r"$\mathbf{x}$", label_offset=(0.28, -0.18))
draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), y, color=HILITE, label=r"$\mathbf{y}$", label_offset=(-0.05, 0.28))
# Third side, x - y, drawn from tip of y to tip of x.
ax.annotate("", xy=x, xytext=y,
            arrowprops=dict(arrowstyle="-|>", color=ACCENT, lw=2.2, shrinkA=0, shrinkB=0))
ax.text(0.5 * (x[0] + y[0]) + 0.15, 0.5 * (x[1] + y[1]) + 0.2,
        r"$\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}$", color=ACCENT, fontsize=13)
# Angle arc at the origin.
th1 = np.arctan2(x[1], x[0]); th2 = np.arctan2(y[1], y[0])
arc = np.linspace(th1, th2, 40)
ax.plot(0.7 * np.cos(arc), 0.7 * np.sin(arc), color=GREY, lw=1.5)
ax.text(0.95 * np.cos(0.5 * (th1 + th2)), 0.95 * np.sin(0.5 * (th1 + th2)),
        r"$\theta$", color=GREY, fontsize=13)
ax.scatter([0], [0], color="black", s=20, zorder=6)
ax.set_xlim(-0.6, 3.6); ax.set_ylim(-0.6, 3.0)
ax.set_aspect("equal")
ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.4); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.4)
ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
ax.set_title("Two vectors, their difference, and the angle between them")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Now run the law of cosines and translate every length into a dot product. The left side is
$c^2 = \lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert^2$. By @eq-norm-sq this is a dot product of the
difference with itself, which we expand using the linearity from @lem-dot-props:
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
= (\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^\top(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}) .
$$
Distribute the dot product over the subtraction in both slots — exactly like FOIL, licensed
term by term by linearity (@lem-dot-props(2)):
$$
(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})^\top(\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y})
= \mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}
 - \mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}
 - \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{x}
 + \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y} .
$$
By symmetry (@lem-dot-props(1)) the two middle terms are equal,
$\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{x}$, so they combine into
$-2\,\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}$. Using @eq-norm-sq on the outer terms,
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
= \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 + \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2 - 2\,\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} .
$$ {#eq-expand}
That is a pure *algebraic* identity — no geometry yet, just the definition and its
linearity. Now lay it beside the law of cosines, which is a pure *geometric* fact about
the same triangle, with $a = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert$, $b = \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$,
$c = \lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert$:
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
= \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 + \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
 - 2\,\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta .
$$ {#eq-lawcos}

The left sides of @eq-expand and @eq-lawcos are the *same quantity*, and the first two
terms on the right match exactly. Subtract those common terms from both equations; what
remains must be equal:
$$
-2\,\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = -2\,\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta .
$$
Divide both sides by $-2$ (a nonzero constant cannot change an equality) and we have the
identity at the heart of the article.

::: {#thm-angle}
**Theorem (geometric form of the dot product).** For nonzero vectors
$\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^n$ with $\theta$ the angle between them,
$$
\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\,\cos\theta .
$$ {#eq-angle}
Equivalently, the angle itself is recovered from coordinates by
$$
\cos\theta = \frac{\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}}{\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert}
= \frac{\sum_i x_i y_i}{\sqrt{\sum_i x_i^2}\,\sqrt{\sum_i y_i^2}} .
$$ {#eq-cos}
:::

::: {.proof}
The computation above @eq-angle is the proof: expanding $\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert^2$
by the algebra of the dot product gives @eq-expand; the law of cosines applied to the same
triangle gives @eq-lawcos; equating the two and cancelling yields
$\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta$. For
@eq-cos, divide @eq-angle by $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$, which is
nonzero because both vectors are nonzero (@lem-dot-props(3)), and substitute the coordinate
formulas @eq-dot-def and @eq-norm-def. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Stand back and appreciate what just happened. @eq-cos lets us compute the angle between two
vectors **from their coordinates alone** — multiply matching entries, sum, and divide by
the two lengths. We never measured an angle; we *computed* one from a spreadsheet. This is
the article's central promise delivered: the geometry the physicists started with is
recovered from the numbers a computer holds.

Two special cases are worth naming on the spot, because the entire next article (GEOM-05)
hangs on the first:

- **Perpendicular ($\theta = 90^\circ$).** Then $\cos\theta = 0$, so @eq-angle forces
  $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = 0$. Conversely, if $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}=0$ for nonzero
  vectors, then $\cos\theta = 0$ and $\theta = 90^\circ$. So **"perpendicular" and "dot
  product zero" are the same statement** — the definition of *orthogonality* that
  [GEOM-05] runs on, and a notion that now makes sense in any dimension, even where we cannot
  picture a right angle.
- **Parallel ($\theta = 0^\circ$ or $180^\circ$).** Then $\cos\theta = \pm 1$, so
  $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = \pm\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$ — the dot
  product hits its largest possible magnitude when the vectors point the same way (or
  exactly opposite). This is the equality case of Cauchy–Schwarz, proved next.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why is the dot product defined as a sum of products and not, say, the product of the
lengths? Which came first — @eq-dot-def or @eq-angle?"* Both formulas compute the same
number, and that equality is the content of @thm-angle. Pedagogically you can start from
either end: physics texts often *define* the dot product geometrically as $\lVert
\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta$ and then *derive* the coordinate sum;
linear-algebra texts (and we, here) *define* it as the coordinate sum and *derive* the
angle. We chose the coordinate-first route deliberately, because in data science the
coordinates are what you are handed — a row of numbers — and the angle is what you want to
extract. The interviewer's deeper point: the two definitions agree, so the dot product is
the unique operation that makes "sum of products" and "magnitude times cosine" the same
thing.
:::

## Cauchy–Schwarz: the cosine is a legal cosine {#sec-cauchy}

There is a gap in @thm-angle we are obligated to close. We wrote $\cos\theta =
\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} / (\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert)$ and called it a
cosine. But a cosine must lie in $[-1, 1]$ — there is no real angle whose cosine is $1.3$.
If the right-hand side of @eq-cos could exceed $1$ in magnitude, the "angle" would be a
fiction. We must prove it never does. That guarantee is the **Cauchy–Schwarz inequality**,
one of the most-used inequalities in all of mathematics.

::: {#thm-cs}
**Theorem (Cauchy–Schwarz).** For all $\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^n$,
$$
\bigl|\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}\bigr| \;\le\; \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert,
$$ {#eq-cs}
with equality if and only if $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ are scalar multiples of each
other (one is a stretched/flipped copy of the other, or one is the zero vector).
:::

::: {.proof}
If $\mathbf{y}=\mathbf{0}$ both sides are $0$ and the inequality holds with equality
($\mathbf{0}$ is a scalar multiple — the $0$ multiple — of anything), so assume
$\mathbf{y}\ne\mathbf{0}$, which makes $\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2 = \mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y}
> 0$ by @lem-dot-props(3).

The trick is to use positivity (@lem-dot-props(3)) on a cleverly chosen vector. For *any*
real number $t$, the vector $\mathbf{x} - t\mathbf{y}$ has nonnegative squared length:
$$
0 \le \lVert\mathbf{x} - t\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
= (\mathbf{x} - t\mathbf{y})^\top(\mathbf{x} - t\mathbf{y}) .
$$
Expand by linearity and symmetry, exactly as in @sec-angle, treating $t$ as a constant
scalar that pulls out of dot products by @lem-dot-props(2):
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x} - t\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
= \mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x} - 2t\,\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} + t^2\,\mathbf{y}^\top\mathbf{y}
= \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 - 2t\,(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}) + t^2 \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2 .
$$
Read the right-hand side as a function of $t$:
$$
q(t) = \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2\, t^2 - 2(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y})\, t + \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 .
$$
This is a quadratic in $t$ with positive leading coefficient $\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2 > 0$
— an upward-opening parabola — and we have just shown $q(t)\ge 0$ for *every* real $t$. The
slickest route is to plug in the $t$ that minimizes $q$, but we can avoid even calculus:
a quadratic $q(t)$ stays $\ge 0$ for all real $t$ if and only if it has no two distinct real
roots, which (for $at^2+bt+c$ with $a>0$) happens exactly when its discriminant
$b^2 - 4ac \le 0$. Here $a = \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2$, $b = -2(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y})$,
$c = \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2$, so
$$
b^2 - 4ac
= 4(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y})^2 - 4\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2
\;\le\; 0 .
$$
Divide by $4$ and rearrange:
$$
(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y})^2 \;\le\; \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2 .
$$
Take the square root of both sides (both are nonnegative, so the inequality survives), and
since $\sqrt{(\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y})^2} = |\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}|$ and
$\sqrt{\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2} =
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$,
$$
|\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}| \le \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\,\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert ,
$$
which is @eq-cs.

*Equality.* The discriminant is exactly zero — equality in @eq-cs — precisely when $q$ has
a (single, repeated) real root $t_\star$, i.e. when $q(t_\star) = \lVert\mathbf{x} -
t_\star\mathbf{y}\rVert^2 = 0$ for some real $t_\star$. By @lem-dot-props(3) a zero norm
forces $\mathbf{x} - t_\star\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{0}$, i.e. $\mathbf{x} = t_\star\mathbf{y}$:
$\mathbf{x}$ is a scalar multiple of $\mathbf{y}$. Conversely, if $\mathbf{x} =
t_\star\mathbf{y}$ then $q(t_\star)=0$ and the discriminant vanishes, giving equality. So
equality holds if and only if the two vectors are parallel. $\qquad\blacksquare$
:::

Cauchy–Schwarz is exactly the missing guarantee. Dividing @eq-cs by the positive number
$\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$ (both vectors nonzero) gives
$$
-1 \le \frac{\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}}{\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert} \le 1 ,
$$
so the quantity we called $\cos\theta$ in @eq-cos really does sit in $[-1, 1]$, and a
genuine angle $\theta\in[0^\circ, 180^\circ]$ exists with that cosine. The angle between two
vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ is now a well-defined object, not a hopeful analogy. And the
equality case has a clean reading: $|\cos\theta| = 1$ — vectors exactly parallel or
anti-parallel — happens precisely when one vector is a scalar multiple of the other, which
is exactly when they are *linearly dependent* in the sense of [GEOM-03]. The geometry
($\theta\in\{0^\circ,180^\circ\}$) and the algebra (linear dependence) agree.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Prove the triangle inequality from Cauchy–Schwarz."* This is a favorite follow-up,
because it shows the inequality earning its keep. The **triangle inequality** says
$\lVert\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}\rVert \le \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert + \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$ —
the direct route is never longer than a detour. Expand the left side squared:
$$
\lVert\mathbf{x}+\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
= \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 + 2\,\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} + \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
\le \lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert^2 + 2\,\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert + \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert^2
= \bigl(\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert + \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\bigr)^2 ,
$$
where the middle step bounds $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} \le |\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y}| \le
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$ by Cauchy–Schwarz. Taking the (nonnegative)
square root of both ends gives the triangle inequality. The single inequality
@eq-cs is what makes our notion of length behave like a real length.
:::

## The inner product: the dot product is one of many {#sec-inner}

We should say honestly what is *standard* about the standard dot product, because the rest
of the series (and a good interview) will press on it. Everything above — the angle
formula, Cauchy–Schwarz, the triangle inequality — used only the three properties in
@lem-dot-props: symmetry, linearity, and positivity. It never used the *specific* recipe
$\sum_i x_i y_i$ beyond those three properties. Any operation that takes two vectors to a
number and obeys those three rules is called an **inner product**, written
$\langle\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\rangle$, and every inner product comes with its own induced
length $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert = \sqrt{\langle\mathbf{x},\mathbf{x}\rangle}$, its own angles,
and its own Cauchy–Schwarz — *because those theorems only ever used the three axioms*.

The dot product $\sum_i x_i y_i$ is the **standard** inner product on $\mathbb{R}^n$, the
one that matches the everyday Euclidean ruler. But it is a *choice*, and sometimes the wrong
one. A simple alternative is a **weighted** inner product $\langle\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}
\rangle_W = \sum_i w_i x_i y_i$ with positive weights $w_i$, which counts some coordinates
as more important than others — and it satisfies all three axioms, so it inherits the entire
geometric apparatus. More general still is $\langle\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y}\rangle_M =
\mathbf{x}^\top M\mathbf{y}$ for a suitable matrix $M$; choosing $M$ to be the inverse
covariance of the data produces the **Mahalanobis** inner product, the right ruler when the
features have wildly different scales and correlations (we will meet it again as a quadratic
form in [GEOM-17] and as the geometry of positive-definite matrices in [GEOM-18]). The
takeaway for now: the *machinery* of length and angle is general; the *standard* dot product
is the particular inner product that treats all coordinates symmetrically and on the same
scale. When that assumption is wrong — and @sec-failure shows when — the standard dot
product is the wrong ruler, and a different inner product is the fix.

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the formulas on real, honest data — including one example near the edge where
the standard dot product is the *wrong* tool, exactly as the constitution demands.

## Example 1 — by hand: length, distance, and angle from coordinates {#sec-ex-hand}

First, the whole apparatus on small vectors, every number in the open, so you watch the
formulas breathe. Take two three-dimensional vectors,
$$
\mathbf{x} = (3, 1, 2), \qquad \mathbf{y} = (1, 4, 0).
$$

```{python}
#| label: ex-hand
#| echo: true
import numpy as np
x = np.array([3.0, 1.0, 2.0])
y = np.array([1.0, 4.0, 0.0])

dot   = x @ y                              # the dot product, sum of products
nx    = np.sqrt(x @ x)                     # ||x||
ny    = np.sqrt(y @ y)                     # ||y||
dist  = np.sqrt((x - y) @ (x - y))         # Euclidean distance ||x - y||
costh = dot / (nx * ny)                    # cos of the angle
theta = np.degrees(np.arccos(costh))       # angle in degrees

print(f"x . y              = {dot:.4f}")
print(f"||x||              = {nx:.4f}")
print(f"||y||              = {ny:.4f}")
print(f"dist(x, y)         = {dist:.4f}")
print(f"cos(theta)         = {costh:.4f}")
print(f"theta (degrees)    = {theta:.2f}")
```

Walk the arithmetic, plugging straight into the boxed formulas:

- **Dot product** (@eq-dot-def): pair and sum,
  $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = (3)(1) + (1)(4) + (2)(0) = 3 + 4 + 0 = 7$.
- **Lengths** (@eq-norm-def): $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert = \sqrt{3^2+1^2+2^2} = \sqrt{14}
  \approx 3.742$ and $\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert = \sqrt{1^2+4^2+0^2} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.123$.
- **Distance** (@eq-distance): $\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y} = (2,-3,2)$, so
  $\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert = \sqrt{2^2 + (-3)^2 + 2^2} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.123$.
- **Angle** (@eq-cos): $\cos\theta = 7 / (\sqrt{14}\,\sqrt{17}) = 7/\sqrt{238} \approx
  0.4537$, giving $\theta \approx 63.0^\circ$.

A positive cosine, so an acute angle: the two vectors broadly agree in direction but are far
from parallel. Notice we obtained a $63^\circ$ angle *in three dimensions* without ever
drawing a protractor — the formula carried all the geometry. The same code, with vectors of
length 10000, would return the angle between two documents just as readily; nothing in the
formulas cares about the dimension.

## Example 2 — cosine similarity: ranking documents by direction, not size {#sec-ex-cosine}

The dot product's headline data-science role is **cosine similarity**: the cosine of the
angle between two feature vectors, used to decide *how alike* two items are. The key idea is
that @eq-cos divides out both lengths, so it measures **direction, not magnitude** — it asks
"do these two vectors point the same way?" and ignores "how big are they?"

That distinction is exactly what we want for text. Represent each document as a vector of
word counts (a "bag of words"): coordinate $i$ is how many times word $i$ appears. A long
article and a short note on the *same topic* use the same words in the same proportions, so
their count vectors point in nearly the same direction — but the long article's vector is
much *longer* (bigger counts). Raw dot products or Euclidean distance would call the two
documents dissimilar simply because one is longer. Cosine similarity, by normalizing away
length, sees that they are about the same thing.

Here are five short documents over a tiny vocabulary. Three are about finance, two about
cooking; one finance document is deliberately much longer than the others.

```{python}
#| label: fig-cosine
#| fig-cap: 'Cosine similarity on bag-of-words vectors. **Left:** the raw word-count matrix; document D3 (''finance, long'') has much larger counts than the rest. **Right:** the matrix of pairwise cosine similarities $\cos\theta = \mathbf{d}_i^\top\mathbf{d}_j / (\lVert\mathbf{d}_i\rVert\lVert\mathbf{d}_j\rVert)$. The three finance documents form a bright block (similarity near 1) and the two cooking documents form another, with near-zero cross-topic similarity. Crucially, the long finance document D3 is still scored as highly similar to the short finance documents D1, D2 — cosine ignored its size and saw only its direction.'
vocab = ["stock", "market", "invest", "recipe", "bake", "flavor"]
docs = {
    "D1 finance":      np.array([4, 3, 2, 0, 0, 0]),
    "D2 finance":      np.array([3, 4, 3, 0, 0, 1]),
    "D3 finance(long)":np.array([40, 35, 28, 1, 0, 2]),
    "D4 cooking":      np.array([0, 0, 0, 5, 4, 3]),
    "D5 cooking":      np.array([1, 0, 0, 4, 5, 4]),
}
names = list(docs.keys())
M = np.array([docs[n] for n in names], dtype=float)

# Cosine similarity matrix.
norms = np.sqrt((M * M).sum(axis=1))
Mn = M / norms[:, None]
C = Mn @ Mn.T

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10.0, 4.0),
                               gridspec_kw={"width_ratios": [1.15, 1]})
imL = axL.imshow(M, cmap="Blues", aspect="auto")
axL.set_xticks(range(len(vocab))); axL.set_xticklabels(vocab, rotation=40, ha="right")
axL.set_yticks(range(len(names))); axL.set_yticklabels(names)
for i in range(M.shape[0]):
    for j in range(M.shape[1]):
        axL.text(j, i, int(M[i, j]), ha="center", va="center",
                 color="white" if M[i, j] > 18 else "black", fontsize=9)
axL.set_title("Raw word counts"); axL.grid(False)

imR = axR.imshow(C, cmap="viridis", vmin=0, vmax=1, aspect="auto")
axR.set_xticks(range(len(names))); axR.set_xticklabels([n.split()[0] for n in names], rotation=40, ha="right")
axR.set_yticks(range(len(names))); axR.set_yticklabels([n.split()[0] for n in names])
for i in range(len(names)):
    for j in range(len(names)):
        axR.text(j, i, f"{C[i, j]:.2f}", ha="center", va="center",
                 color="white" if C[i, j] < 0.6 else "black", fontsize=9)
axR.set_title("Pairwise cosine similarity"); axR.grid(False)
fig.colorbar(imR, ax=axR, fraction=0.046, pad=0.04)
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"cos(D1, D3 long) = {C[0,2]:.3f}   (same topic, very different size -> still high)")
print(f"cos(D1, D4)      = {C[0,3]:.3f}   (different topic -> near zero)")
print(f"Euclidean dist(D1, D3 long) = {np.sqrt(((M[0]-M[2])**2).sum()):.1f}  (size makes them look far!)")
```

The printout makes the point sharp. Documents D1 and D3 are about the same topic, but D3 is
ten times longer; their **Euclidean distance is enormous**, which would wrongly rank them
as dissimilar. Their **cosine similarity is near 1**, correctly recognizing that they point
the same way. This length-invariance is why cosine similarity, built from nothing but the
dot product and two norms, is the default measure in search engines, document retrieval,
and the nearest-neighbor step of many recommender systems.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When would you use cosine similarity instead of Euclidean distance?"* When **direction
matters and magnitude is a nuisance** — text where document length varies, user-rating
vectors where some users rate far more items than others, any setting where you care about
*proportions* across features rather than their absolute sizes. Use Euclidean distance when
magnitude is meaningful (physical measurements, positions in space). The crisp connection
to state: on vectors already normalized to unit length, the two are monotone equivalents,
because $\lVert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\rVert^2 = 2 - 2\,\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = 2(1 -
\cos\theta)$ when $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert = \lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert = 1$ — so on the unit
sphere, larger cosine *is* smaller distance. Off the unit sphere they can disagree, and the
disagreement is exactly the document-length effect we just saw.
:::

## Example 3 (edge of applicability) — when the standard dot product is the wrong ruler {#sec-ex-edge}

Now the honest boundary. The standard dot product treats every coordinate identically and
on the same scale. When the features have **wildly different units or scales**, that
even-handedness is a bug, not a feature: the largest-scale coordinate dominates the sum of
products and silently drowns out the others. The "angle" you compute is then governed by an
arbitrary choice of units.

Consider two houses, each described by `(price in dollars, number of bedrooms)`. Price runs
to hundreds of thousands; bedrooms run from 1 to 5. We will compute the angle between two
houses that differ a lot in bedrooms but little in price, and then watch what happens when
we merely **change the price units** from dollars to thousands of dollars — a change that
should not affect any honest notion of "how similar are these houses," yet swings the dot
product geometry wildly.

```{python}
#| label: fig-edge
#| fig-cap: 'The edge of applicability. Two houses described by (price, bedrooms). **Left, in raw units (dollars):** price is so large it dominates the dot product; the two houses look almost perfectly aligned (angle near $0^\circ$) no matter how their bedrooms differ. **Middle, price in $1000s:** the *same houses*, only the price unit changed, now show a far larger angle. The standard dot product gave a unit-dependent, meaningless answer. **Right, after standardizing each feature to comparable scale:** the angle finally reflects the real difference between the houses. The lesson: the standard dot product silently assumes the coordinates are on comparable scales.'
def angle_deg(a, b):
    return np.degrees(np.arccos((a @ b) / (np.sqrt(a @ a) * np.sqrt(b @ b))))

# Two houses: similar price, different bedroom count.
h1_dollars = np.array([320_000.0, 2.0])
h2_dollars = np.array([330_000.0, 5.0])

# Same houses, price expressed in thousands of dollars.
h1_k = np.array([320.0, 2.0])
h2_k = np.array([330.0, 5.0])

# Standardized: divide each feature by a representative spread so units vanish.
price_sd, bed_sd = 50_000.0, 1.5
h1_z = np.array([320_000.0 / price_sd, 2.0 / bed_sd])
h2_z = np.array([330_000.0 / price_sd, 5.0 / bed_sd])

cases = [
    ("raw: dollars",   h1_dollars, h2_dollars),
    ("price in $1000s", h1_k,      h2_k),
    ("standardized",   h1_z,       h2_z),
]
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(10.2, 3.5))
for ax, (title, a, b) in zip(axes, cases):
    an = a / np.sqrt(a @ a)   # show directions only
    bn = b / np.sqrt(b @ b)
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), an, color=VEC, label="house 1", label_offset=(0.0, 0.12))
    draw_vec(ax, (0, 0), bn, color=HILITE, label="house 2", label_offset=(0.0, -0.16))
    ang = angle_deg(a, b)
    ax.set_title(f"{title}\nangle = {ang:.1f}°", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlim(-0.2, 1.2); ax.set_ylim(-0.2, 1.2)
    ax.set_aspect("equal")
    ax.axhline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.4); ax.axvline(0, color=GREY, lw=0.8, alpha=0.4)
    ax.set_xticks([]); ax.set_yticks([])
plt.tight_layout()

print(f"angle, dollars     = {angle_deg(h1_dollars, h2_dollars):6.2f} deg")
print(f"angle, $1000s      = {angle_deg(h1_k,       h2_k):6.2f} deg")
print(f"angle, standardized= {angle_deg(h1_z,       h2_z):6.2f} deg")
```

The three angles are wildly different for **the same two houses**. In raw dollars the price
coordinate is so enormous that the sum of products is essentially all price, the bedroom
difference is invisible, and the houses look nearly identical in direction (angle near
$0^\circ$). Re-express price in thousands — a change of units that carries no information —
and the angle leaps, because now price and bedrooms are closer in scale. The geometry was
hostage to an arbitrary unit choice.

**When you reach for something else.** This is the standard dot product used outside its
assumption. The fixes are exactly the more general inner products of @sec-inner:

- **Standardize first.** Rescale each feature (e.g. divide by its standard deviation) so the
  coordinates are dimensionless and comparable, *then* take the ordinary dot product. The
  right panel does this and recovers an honest angle. This is the most common practical fix
  and is why "standardize your features" is a reflex before any distance- or angle-based
  method (k-NN, k-means, PCA).
- **Use a weighted or Mahalanobis inner product** (@sec-inner), which builds the rescaling —
  and, in the Mahalanobis case, the *correlations* between features — directly into the
  ruler. This is the principled version of standardizing, developed via positive-definite
  matrices in [GEOM-18].

The honest summary: the dot product is a *ruler that assumes all axes are marked in the same
units*. When that is false, fix the axes (standardize) or change the ruler (weighted inner
product). Never compute angles on raw, mixed-unit features and trust them.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

Collecting the boundaries in one place — the section that separates "can compute a dot
product" from "knows when the number means something."

## Scale sensitivity {#sec-fail-scale}

As @sec-ex-edge showed in full, the standard dot product is **not scale-invariant across
features**. A coordinate measured in large units dominates the sum of products and the
induced angle. Whenever features carry different units or spreads, standardize them or use a
weighted/Mahalanobis inner product before computing lengths, distances, or angles. This is
the single most common way the dot product is misused in practice.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**Computing cosine similarity or Euclidean distance on raw, mixed-scale features.** The
result is governed by your arbitrary choice of units (dollars vs. thousands, grams vs.
kilograms), not by the data. Always standardize features to a comparable scale first — or
adopt an inner product (weighted, Mahalanobis) that encodes the right scale. The dot product
gives you a number on *any* input; whether that number is *meaningful* is your
responsibility, not the formula's.
:::

## High dimensions: the angle concentrates {#sec-fail-highdim}

A subtler, genuinely surprising failure shows up when the dimension $n$ is large — the home
turf of data science. Take two **random** vectors in $\mathbb{R}^n$ with independent
coordinates centered at zero. As $n$ grows, the angle between them concentrates sharply
around $90^\circ$: random high-dimensional vectors are *almost always nearly orthogonal*.
The reason is visible in @eq-cos: the numerator $\sum_i x_i y_i$ is a sum of $n$ terms with
random signs that largely cancel (it grows like $\sqrt{n}$), while each norm in the
denominator grows like $\sqrt{n}$, so the cosine behaves like $\sqrt{n}/n = 1/\sqrt{n} \to
0$. @fig-concentration shows the angle distribution collapsing onto $90^\circ$ as $n$ rises.

```{python}
#| label: fig-concentration
#| fig-cap: 'The concentration of angle in high dimensions. For each dimension $n$, we draw many pairs of random vectors (independent standard-normal coordinates) and histogram the angle between them. In low dimensions the angle is spread out, but as $n$ grows the distribution piles up tightly around $90^\circ$: almost every pair of random high-dimensional vectors is nearly orthogonal. This is a face of the ''curse of dimensionality'' and a reason cosine similarity loses discriminating power when features are many and largely uninformative.'
rng = np.random.default_rng(0)
dims = [2, 10, 100, 1000]
colors = [PALE, ACCENT, VEC, HILITE]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(6.6, 4.0))
for n, col in zip(dims, colors):
    A = rng.standard_normal((4000, n))
    B = rng.standard_normal((4000, n))
    dots = np.sum(A * B, axis=1)
    angs = np.degrees(np.arccos(dots / (np.linalg.norm(A, axis=1) * np.linalg.norm(B, axis=1))))
    ax.hist(angs, bins=60, density=True, histtype="step", lw=2.0, color=col, label=f"$n={n}$")
ax.axvline(90, color=GREY, lw=1.2, ls="--")
ax.set_xlabel("angle between two random vectors (degrees)")
ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Random vectors become nearly orthogonal as $n$ grows")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
```

The practical consequence: in very high dimensions, *most* pairs look nearly orthogonal, so
the cosine between genuinely related items must compete against a sea of near-$90^\circ$
noise. Cosine similarity still works — the signal is real — but its *contrast* shrinks, and
this is one face of the **curse of dimensionality**. The usual remedies are to first reduce
dimension (PCA, owned by Unsupervised Learning, built on the SVD of [GEOM-20]) or to use
features that are informative rather than padded, so the relevant directions are not buried.
The point for this article: the dot product remains exactly correct, but *what counts as a
"large" similarity* must be calibrated against this baseline, not against the naive
intuition that $90^\circ$ means "unrelated."

## The zero vector has no direction {#sec-fail-zero}

A small but real edge: the angle formula @eq-cos divides by $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert
\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert$, which is zero if either vector is the zero vector. This is not a
formula bug — it is the honest statement that **the zero vector has no direction**, so the
"angle to it" is undefined. In code, a document with no words in your vocabulary, or a user
who rated nothing, produces a zero vector, and cosine similarity is genuinely undefined for
it. Guard against it explicitly (e.g. drop or specially handle empty vectors) rather than
letting a divide-by-zero produce a silent `nan`.

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived from [GEOM-01 *Adding and Scaling*] with only affine structure — addition and
scaling, but no ruler and no protractor. This article installed both from one operation, the
dot product: length via $\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert = \sqrt{\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x}}$
(Pythagoras in $n$ dimensions) and angle via $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} =
\lVert\mathbf{x}\rVert\lVert\mathbf{y}\rVert\cos\theta$ (the law of cosines), with
Cauchy–Schwarz guaranteeing the angle is real. From here:

- **[GEOM-05 *Orthogonality and Orthonormal Bases*]** — the read-as-a-pair companion to this
  article. It runs entirely on the special case we flagged: $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{y} = 0$
  means perpendicular. It builds the generalized Pythagorean theorem, orthonormal bases
  (where coordinates are *just dot products*, $c_i = \mathbf{q}_i^\top\mathbf{x}$), and the
  orthogonal complement.
- **[GEOM-06 *Orthogonal Projection onto a Subspace*]** — the keystone of the track. The
  projection of one vector onto another, $(\mathbf{a}^\top\mathbf{x}/\mathbf{a}^\top
  \mathbf{a})\,\mathbf{a}$, is built entirely from the dot products defined here; "closest
  point" is "drop a perpendicular," and perpendicular is dot-product-zero. This is the
  geometry under least squares.
- **[GEOM-17 *Quadratic Forms*]** — generalizes the squared length
  $\mathbf{x}^\top\mathbf{x} = \sum_i x_i^2$ to $\mathbf{x}^\top A\mathbf{x}$, and the
  weighted/Mahalanobis inner products of @sec-inner are exactly quadratic forms in disguise.
- **Cross-track:** cosine similarity (@sec-ex-cosine) is consumed by Classification (k-NN,
  the kernel trick, where a kernel is a generalized inner product) and Unsupervised Learning
  (clustering, recommender systems). The "regression slope is covariance over variance"
  result of [REGR-01] is, geometrically, a dot product in disguise — a connection [REGR-04]
  makes explicit once projection is in hand.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Historical thread.** The dot product's modern form was distilled by J. W. Gibbs and
  Oliver Heaviside in the 1880s out of Hamilton's quaternion calculus; Gibbs's lecture notes
  *Elements of Vector Analysis* (1881–84) are where the dot and cross products first appear
  as the stand-alone operations taught today. The notion of "work as force-dot-displacement"
  is the physical seed the whole construction grew from.
- **Cauchy–Schwarz.** The inequality is named for Augustin-Louis Cauchy (1821, finite sums)
  and Hermann Schwarz (1888, integrals); Viktor Bunyakovsky proved the integral form
  earlier, so it is sometimes the Cauchy–Bunyakovsky–Schwarz inequality. Our quadratic-
  discriminant proof is the standard one and the same template that proves the general
  inner-product version in @sec-inner verbatim.
- **Open threads this article deliberately deferred.** *What if perpendicular is the whole
  story?* — orthogonality and its consequences are [GEOM-05]. *How do we find the closest
  point in a subspace?* — projection, [GEOM-06], the keystone. *Is there a "best" inner
  product for a given dataset?* — the Mahalanobis distance and positive-definite matrices,
  [GEOM-17]–[GEOM-18]. *Why do nearly all high-dimensional vectors look orthogonal, made
  precise?* — measure concentration, a probabilistic story owned by the Probability track but
  visible here in @fig-concentration.
